+ Teen horror: Tick Tock (Goosebumps) — Score: 70.0
Prompt:
Teen Horror (Goosebumps style) Write a chapter where a middle school student discovers their new smart watch has the ability to rewind time, but doesn't realize that each use is aging them until they notice physical changes (like accelerating through puberty) and that people are treating them differently. They inadvertently cause a timeline shift with disastrous consequences -- and it can only be rectified (maybe?) by reversing the clock once more. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
### The Thirteenth Hour The watch arrived in a box with no return address. It sat on our porch between Mom’s flowerpots, wrapped in shiny black paper. My name—**DYLAN REYES**—was written across the top in silver ink. “Early birthday present?” Mom guessed. My birthday was three months away. Inside was a smartwatch with a round black face and a silver button on the side. No instructions. No charger. No company logo. When I strapped it on, the screen lit up. **12:47:33** Below the time was a tiny curved arrow. I tapped it. Nothing happened. Then the watch vibrated, and a message flashed: **TURN BACK?** “Sure,” I muttered. The second hand spun backward. The kitchen lights flickered. Suddenly Mom was standing by the refrigerator instead of the sink. “Early birthday present?” she asked. I froze. She was holding the same carton of orange juice she had put away a minute ago. I looked at the watch. **12:47:33** No way. I tested it again after school the next day. I was hurrying past the bike rack when I bumped into Lucas Bell. His volcano project fell and cracked down the middle. “You ruined it!” he yelled. Before I could answer, I pressed the silver button. **TURN BACK?** The hands reversed. The world stretched like melted cheese. Then I was three minutes in the past, walking toward the bike rack. This time I stepped aside. Lucas passed safely. A gray stray cat watched from under a bush. It had one white ear and bright green eyes. As I walked by, it made a strange chirping sound. Like it recognized me. By Friday, I was using the watch for everything. I rewound a pop quiz and changed my answers. I rewound lunch after dropping my pizza. I rewound basketball tryouts until I sank the final shot. Each time, the gray cat appeared nearby. Under a car. On a fence. Beside the gym doors. Watching. After the twelfth rewind, it followed me all the way home. That night, I noticed a dark shadow above my upper lip. I scrubbed it with soap. It didn’t come off. At breakfast, Mom stared at me. “You look different.” “I’m tired.” “No.” She tilted her head. “Older.” At school, my jeans felt too short. My sneakers pinched my toes. When I answered attendance, my voice came out deep and rough. Everyone laughed. Even my teacher looked startled. By lunch, my knees hurt. I locked myself in a bathroom stall and checked the watch. A number glowed beneath the time. **AGE BALANCE: +2 YEARS, 4 MONTHS** My mouth went dry. I was twelve years old. But in the mirror, I looked fourteen. Maybe fifteen. My cheeks were sharper. My hair seemed darker. Pimples dotted my forehead. The bathroom door opened. “Dylan?” Mr. Pike called. “Students aren’t supposed to be in here.” “I am a student!” He stared at me. “Do you have identification?” I ran. Outside, the gray cat sat in the middle of the sidewalk. “You know, don’t you?” I whispered. It blinked slowly. I pressed the watch button, thinking I could undo the last rewind. The message was different. **REVERSE CLOCK?** “That’s what I want.” The cat hissed. I pressed YES. Everything went black. When I opened my eyes, I was standing in front of school. Rain poured from a purple-gray sky. The school looked wrong. Half the windows were broken. Weeds pushed through cracks in the pavement. A rusted sign hung over the entrance: **REYES MEMORIAL SHELTER** My name. A siren wailed somewhere nearby. I rushed inside. The hallway was filled with cots. Adults in dirty coats stared at me. A woman grabbed my arm. “Dylan?” she gasped. It was Mom. But her hair was almost completely gray. She looked sixty. “You’re alive,” she whispered. “After all these years…” “All what years?” A TV in the corner showed footage of smoking buildings. Words crawled across the bottom: **BLACKOUT CRISIS ENTERS ELEVENTH YEAR** Mom began crying. “You disappeared the day the power grid failed. They said you caused it.” “What?” A man turned up the volume. Onscreen, security footage showed someone in a school bathroom. Someone tall, with my face, pressing a glowing watch. Then came a flash. Every light in the city went dark. “No,” I whispered. Mom gripped my shoulders. “Where have you been?” I looked at the watch. **AGE BALANCE: +13 YEARS, 1 MONTH** My reflection stared from the dark TV screen. I wasn’t a kid anymore. I looked twenty-five. The shelter doors slammed open. Two uniformed officers entered. “There!” one shouted. “The Reyes boy!” Boy? They raised strange metal weapons. I ran. Outside, the rain stung my face. The gray cat waited beneath the rusted sign. It looked exactly the same. White ear. Green eyes. “You followed me,” I said. It trotted toward an alley, glancing back until I followed. We reached a dead end blocked by twisted pipes. The cat jumped onto a crate. Scratched into the wall behind it was a picture of a clock. Underneath were four words: **TIME TAKES WHAT YOU TAKE.** The officers’ boots splashed closer. “Stop!” someone shouted. The watch showed one final message. **RETURN TO FIRST MOMENT?** Beneath it: **COST UNKNOWN** My thumb hovered over YES. If I went back to the porch, maybe I could leave the box unopened. Maybe the blackout would never happen. Maybe I’d be twelve again. Or maybe “cost unknown” meant I’d turn into an old man. Or dust. The cat placed one paw on my wrist. For the first time, it spoke. Its voice was soft and dry. “You always choose yes.” I stared at it. “Always?” Behind me, the officers entered the alley. I pressed the button. The world screamed backward. Rain flew into the clouds. Buildings rebuilt themselves. My bones twisted. My skin tightened, loosened, and tightened again. Then— I stood on our porch. A black box waited between Mom’s flowerpots. My hands were small. I was twelve. Across the street, the gray cat sat on the sidewalk. I kicked the box off the porch. It landed in the bushes. The cat’s green eyes widened. From inside the box came a muffled vibration. Then my wrist buzzed. I looked down. The watch was already strapped to my arm. Its face lit up. **12:47:33** Below it appeared a new message: **WELCOME BACK, DYLAN.** And from the kitchen, Mom called, “Who are you?”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Goosebumps-style teen horror prompt, incorporating all required elements: a middle schooler with a time-rewinding smartwatch, physical aging consequences, social alienation, timeline disruption, and a final reversal attempt. The story maintains an appropriately dark tone throughout while capturing the accessible horror style of R.L. Stine. The narrative structure is well-paced, building tension through escalating consequences. The protagonist's initial casual use of the watch feels believable for a middle schooler, and his growing panic as he discovers the aging effects creates genuine suspense. The mysterious gray cat serves as an effective harbinger and witness to the temporal disturbances. The imagery is consistently strong, particularly the dystopian future sequence with the "REYES MEMORIAL SHELTER" and the protagonist's mother aged beyond recognition. The visual of the watch messages and the scratched clock symbol in the alley create memorable, unsettling moments. The prose maintains clarity while building atmosphere effectively. However, the piece suffers from some significant weaknesses. The dialogue, while functional, lacks distinctiveness - characters speak in fairly generic ways that don't reveal much personality. The mother's reactions feel somewhat flat given the extraordinary circumstances. The mysterious cat's single line of dialogue ("You always choose yes") hints at deeper complexity but isn't fully developed. The ending presents the story's most problematic element. While the cyclical nature creates appropriate horror, the final revelation that the watch is inescapable feels somewhat unearned. The transition from the protagonist successfully returning to the past to suddenly finding the watch already on his wrist lacks sufficient setup. The mother's final question ("Who are you?") suggests interesting implications but comes too abruptly. The characterization remains relatively surface-level. Dylan reacts believably to events but doesn't develop much personality beyond his predicament. His decision-making process could be more nuanced, and his emotional journey more complex. The story tells us he's panicking rather than fully showing his psychological deterioration. Some elements feel predictable within the genre - the mysterious package, the escalating supernatural consequences, the failed attempt to escape fate. While executed competently, these don't offer particularly fresh takes on familiar horror tropes. The writing occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in describing Dylan's emotional states and the passage of time. The pacing in the middle section rushes through several rewinds without fully exploring their psychological impact. Despite these limitations, the story succeeds in creating an engaging horror narrative with genuine tension and a satisfyingly dark conclusion that fits the genre expectations. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 11 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 14

+ Noir romance: Carnival of Dangerous Intentions — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Carnival of Dangerous Intentions Coney Island, New York, July 1937. She stopped just before reaching the shooting gallery set up along the boardwalk. It was near dusk, and the carnival lights were buzzing on. **His locks**. The words flashed through her mind, again, like an intruder. Such an old-fashioned turn of phrase that kept surfacing, and something else -- dangerously compelling -- quickly suppressed, lest she question why she had returned here at all. This place where she had promised to meet the stranger -- the young man with the obsidian curls and even darker eyes. She didn't even know his name. Write a scene in a larger story inspired by this prompt. The scene should capture an atmosphere that blends romantic allure with an undercurrent of subtle danger or moral ambiguity. Think 50 shades, but with a more restrained classic sensibility. The scene is all about establishing tension. Write for the feminine perspective. Include roughly 50% dialogue, 20% internal monologue, and 30% exposition and scene setting. The tone should have some edge to it. 1000 words.
Model Output:
She stopped just before the shooting gallery, where tin ducks traveled in patient circles beneath a striped awning. Near dusk, the boardwalk seemed caught between two tempers. Out over the water, the last light lay pale and innocent along the waves. Behind her, Coney Island kindled itself by degrees: red bulbs around the Ferris wheel, violet tubes above the dance hall, and the new sodium lamps that gave everything beneath them a jaundiced, theatrical cast. One of those lamps flickered over the gallery. In the instant of darkness, she saw him only as a silhouette. Then the lamp buzzed awake, and there he was. His locks. The phrase entered her mind again, absurdly antique and far too intimate. Obsidian curls lay disobediently against his forehead. His suit was charcoal, his tie black, his hat held loosely at his side. He looked as if he had come to a funeral and taken a wrong turn into summer. “You’re late,” he said. “I nearly didn’t come.” “That isn’t the same thing.” “No.” Evelyn drew off one glove, finger by finger, though she could not have said why. “I suppose it isn’t.” His gaze followed the movement. Not crudely. That would have been easier to dismiss. He watched with the attention of a man committing something to memory. The gallery barked and clattered behind them. A boy fired three times and missed three times. The proprietor shouted encouragement in a voice made hoarse by repetition. “You still haven’t told me your name,” she said. “You still haven’t asked the right question.” “And what question is that?” He stepped beneath the wavering lamp. Light crossed his face in fragments—cheekbone, mouth, one dark eye—then vanished again. “You should ask why I chose you.” A chill went through her that the July air did not justify. She had spent three days telling herself she’d returned because of the envelope in her handbag. Because of the photograph inside it. Because curiosity, properly disciplined, was not a vice. She had not returned for his voice, or the unsettling courtesy with which he had touched the brim of his hat when they first met, or the sense that he had seen through every careful layer of her. “Perhaps I don’t care,” she said. “You care very much, Miss Vale.” Her bare hand tightened around the glove. “I never gave you my surname.” “No.” “Then how do you know it?” “You’re standing too far away.” “That is not an answer.” “It’s an invitation.” The sodium light failed. Darkness pressed close, filled with calliope music, surf, and the sharp report of rifles. When the lamp returned, his expression had altered. Or perhaps the strange light only made it seem so. Evelyn approached until the gallery counter stood between them. The distance was proper. Almost. “Now answer me.” “My name is Adrian Thorne.” “That sounds invented.” “So does Evelyn Vale.” “It happens to be mine.” “So does mine.” The proprietor swept spent pellets from the counter. “Try your luck, miss? Win yourself a prize. Fella looks like he could use a teddy bear.” Adrian placed a coin on the wood. “She’ll shoot,” he said. Evelyn looked at him. “Will I?” “If you want the answer.” The proprietor slid a rifle toward her. Its stock was smooth from a thousand hands, its metal cool and slightly greasy. “This is ridiculous.” “Most tests are.” “What does shooting a tin bird prove?” “That depends upon which bird you choose.” Five ducks revolved at the back of the booth, painted yellow, blue, white, red, and black. Above them hung cheap prizes: plaster dogs, stuffed cats, dolls with startled faces. Adrian leaned near enough for her to catch the clean scent of his soap beneath tobacco and salt air. “Not the red one,” he murmured. She turned her head. “Why?” “Because everyone chooses red.” “Then the black one?” “That’s what you think I want.” His mouth was close to her temple. He had not touched her, yet the absence of contact felt deliberate—as if restraint were another instrument he knew how to use. She disliked him for understanding that. She disliked herself more for remaining still. “Which one, then?” she asked. “The white.” “It’s the easiest.” “Yes.” Evelyn lifted the rifle. Her father had taught her to shoot behind their house in Connecticut, before her mother decided the skill was unbecoming. Steady the breath. Do not snatch the trigger. Never point at anything you cannot bear to destroy. The white duck entered the sights. She fired. The duck snapped backward with a metallic ping. Adrian smiled. “Again.” She struck it a second time. “Again.” The third shot sent it rattling down behind the painted scenery. The proprietor whistled. “Lady’s got an eye. Pick a prize.” “The doll,” Adrian said. “I can choose for myself.” “I’m sure you can.” She selected a small stuffed fox instead. Its fur was the color of rust, one glass eye set higher than the other. Adrian accepted it from the proprietor before she could, then handed it to her with grave ceremony. “Contrary,” he said. “Presumptuous.” “Afraid?” “No.” “That was too quick.” The word *afraid* hung between them. Evelyn felt the envelope in her handbag as though it had grown heavier. “What does the photograph mean?” she asked. “Not here.” “You brought me here.” “I brought you where we could be seen.” “By whom?” His eyes moved past her shoulder. She resisted the urge to look. The boardwalk crowd flowed around them: sailors and shopgirls, families, men in summer suits. Under the faltering lamps, each face appeared and disappeared in broken sequence. Any one of them might have been watching. No one seemed to be. “That man in the photograph,” Evelyn said. “He’s dead.” “Yes.” “You knew him?” “Yes.” “So did my husband.” “I know.” The word struck more cleanly than the rifle’s report. She turned on him. “What do you know about Henry?” “Enough to wonder why he sent his wife to identify a body he claimed never to have seen.” “He didn’t send me.” “No?” Adrian’s gaze dropped briefly to her handbag. “Then who told you to go to the morgue?” The carnival spun around her in lurid bands of light. She remembered the anonymous telephone call, the clerk’s averted eyes, the sheet drawn back from a face mottled by river water. She remembered the dead man’s signet ring—and seeing its twin in Henry’s desk. “I won’t discuss my husband with a stranger.” “Then stop making me one.” The audacity of it stole her reply. Adrian moved around the counter. He did it slowly, allowing her ample time to step away. She did not. When he reached for her, it was only to turn her bare hand palm upward. His fingers closed around her wrist with measured pressure. Not force. Not quite. Her pulse leapt beneath his thumb. “Let go,” she said. “Do you mean it?” She met his eyes. They were darker than she remembered, or the light made them so. “Yes.” He released her instantly. The obedience was more disquieting than refusal might have been. “You enjoy that,” she said. “What?” “Making people uncertain.” “No. I enjoy discovering what they do with uncertainty.” “And what have I done?” “Come closer.” She realized that she had. The rifle cracked behind them. The sodium lamp overhead sputtered, plunging Adrian’s face into shadow. For one breath, she could imagine him as anything: criminal, savior, liar, the instrument of her ruin. When the light returned, he was watching her mouth. “Walk with me,” he said. “Where?” “Somewhere Henry’s man cannot hear us.” Her composure cracked. She glanced over her shoulder. Across the boardwalk, beside a saltwater-taffy stand, a broad man in a straw hat abruptly turned away. Evelyn faced Adrian again. “You arranged this.” “I arranged very little. That is the problem.” “Why should I trust you?” “You shouldn’t.” The answer, quiet and immediate, drew her more powerfully than any assurance could have. He offered his arm. Beyond him, the amusement park blazed against the darkening sea, beautiful in the gaudy, temporary manner of things built to distract people from danger. Music swelled from the dance pavilion. The lamp flickered once, twice, laying prison-bar shadows across Adrian’s face. Evelyn tucked the fox beneath her arm. Then she placed her gloved hand lightly on his sleeve. “If this is a trap,” she said, “you’ll regret underestimating me.” His smile held no comfort. “My dear Miss Vale,” he murmured, guiding her into the crowd, “that is the one mistake I have no intention of making.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craftsmanship in creating atmospheric tension and romantic intrigue. The author successfully captures the requested blend of classic sensibility with subtle danger, establishing a noir-like atmosphere through careful attention to setting and mood. The 1937 Coney Island backdrop is vividly rendered with period-appropriate details like sodium lamps, calliope music, and the carnival's gaudy theatricality. The dialogue crackles with tension and subtext, particularly in exchanges like "You still haven't asked the right question" and the shooting gallery sequence. The conversation feels natural while maintaining an undercurrent of mystery and sexual tension. The author skillfully uses the shooting gallery as both literal action and metaphor for the psychological game being played between the characters. Character development is nuanced, particularly Evelyn's internal conflict between attraction and wariness. Her thoughts reveal layers of motivation while maintaining mystery about her true circumstances. Adrian is appropriately enigmatic without becoming a cipher - his restraint and calculated responses suggest depth rather than mere mystique. The prose strikes an effective balance between atmospheric description and forward momentum. Sentences like "His suit was charcoal, his tie black, his hat held loosely at his side. He looked as if he had come to a funeral and taken a wrong turn into summer" demonstrate elegant imagery without excessive ornamentation. The piece adheres well to the structural requirements, maintaining roughly the requested dialogue/monologue/exposition ratios. The feminine perspective is convincingly maintained throughout, and the moral ambiguity emerges naturally through plot revelations about the photograph, dead man, and husband's potential involvement. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the period voice slips slightly modern, and a few instances where the mystery elements feel somewhat familiar to the genre. However, the execution elevates these conventional elements through skilled writing and character development. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t continue in Stephen King’s exact style, but I can write an original chapter with grounded small-town realism, slow-building psychological dread, isolation, and surreal horror. --- By the time they got back to the lodge, snow had begun ticking against the windows. Not falling, exactly. The flakes were hard and dry, little white seeds blown sideways out of a sky the color of old dishwater. They struck the glass and clung there, trembling. JT sat at the kitchen table with his hands wrapped around a mug of coffee he was too young to need and too tired not to drink. His father stood at the sink, washing blood from a hunting knife. Mike was feeding split birch into the woodstove. Nobody mentioned the buck. That was what made it worse. “You drag your ass all day like that tomorrow,” Mike said, “we’re tying a bell on you.” JT looked down into his coffee. “Boot came loose.” “Your boots are always coming loose.” “Leave him be,” Dad said. Mike glanced over. “I’m just saying.” Their father held the knife beneath the running tap. Pink water curled around the drain. He stared at it with such concentration that JT wondered if he was seeing something in the basin that the rest of them couldn’t. Then Dad shut off the faucet. The silence that followed seemed too large for the room. “You boys ever hear about the Bell cabin?” he asked. Mike laughed once. “Here we go.” JT knew the name. Everybody did, though nobody seemed to agree on who Bell had been. A trapper. A prospector. A drunk from Quebec who had murdered his partner over canned peaches. The cabin stood six or seven miles northwest of the lodge, past the old burn, where no maintained trail went anymore. It had been abandoned for longer than JT had been alive. Depending on who told the story, something still lived there. Dad wiped the knife on a towel. “Saw smoke from that direction.” “When?” JT asked. Their father looked at him, and for a moment his expression was blank. “Today.” “There’s no chimney left,” Mike said. “Roof caved in, too.” “So you’ve been there?” “No. Denny has.” “Denny says a lot.” Mike closed the stove door harder than necessary. “You’re the one bringing up ghost cabins.” “I didn’t say ghost.” He said it mildly, but JT felt cold move through him. Outside, the snow ticked against the glass. That night, JT woke to someone walking on the porch. He lay still under three quilts, listening. Step. Pause. Step. The boards complained softly beneath the weight. Not the quick, nervous feet of a fox. Not the heavy shuffle of his father going out to piss. This was slow. Deliberate. Each footfall landed only after the last echo had faded. JT looked toward Mike’s bed. His brother was a shape beneath the blankets, facing the wall. “Mike,” JT whispered. The footsteps stopped. JT held his breath. Something brushed the outside of the bedroom wall. It made a dry, fibrous sound, like antlers dragged across cedar shingles. Mike spoke without turning. “Go to sleep.” “You hear that?” “No.” “You’re awake.” “Because you’re talking.” On the porch, a board creaked directly beneath the window. JT stared at the curtains. Moonlight—or snowlight—leaked around their edges. He imagined a face on the other side. Not a caribou face. Not exactly. Something patient enough to wait until he chose to look. “Mike?” “What?” “What happened in the clearing?” His brother rolled over. Even in the dark, JT could see his eyes were open. “What clearing?” JT didn’t answer. After a while, Mike said, “You ought to quit making things up.” Then he turned back to the wall. In the morning, there were tracks around the lodge. His father found them first. He crouched beside the porch steps, hat pushed back, studying the long slots pressed into the new snow. “Buck,” Mike said. “Caribou don’t come this close,” JT said. Mike gave him a look. “They go where they want.” The tracks circled the lodge three times. JT counted. On the third circuit, they changed. The front pair remained hoofprints. The rear impressions lengthened until they resembled bare human feet, though each was narrow and split down the middle. JT blinked hard. When he looked again, wind had feathered the edges. They were only tracks. “You see smoke again?” Mike asked Dad. Their father was gazing northwest. “No,” he said. “But we’ll check.” JT’s stomach tightened. “Check what?” “The Bell place.” Mike grinned, but it was forced. “Maybe the ghost has coffee.” They left after breakfast. The old burn began where the spruce thinned into blackened poles. A fire had passed through twelve years before, yet the land still looked wounded. Pale saplings rose among charred stumps. The snow covered the ground unevenly, exposing strips of black earth that seemed warm despite the cold. Nobody talked much. JT followed behind, as usual. His father walked with purpose, though there was no visible trail. Twice Mike asked if he was sure of the direction. Twice Dad ignored him. Around noon they found the first antler. It stood upright in the snow, all its tines buried except one. A hundred yards farther on, they found another. Then another. They formed a line through the burned woods. “People set these?” JT asked. Mike nudged one with his boot. It didn’t move. “Frozen in.” His father kept walking. The cabin appeared without warning. One moment there were only trees; the next it stood between them, small and black, its roof intact. JT stopped. He had seen photographs of the Bell cabin tacked to the wall of the trading post. In those pictures the roof had collapsed inward and young birches grew through the floor. Here the cedar shakes looked wet and newly laid. A thin thread of smoke rose from the chimney. “Dad,” JT said. Their father approached the door. Mike caught his arm. “Hold up.” From inside came a dull knock. Three times. JT thought of knuckles against wood. Then he thought of hooves. Dad pulled free. “Someone could need help.” “Someone could shoot you,” Mike said. Another three knocks. The cabin door eased inward. Darkness waited beyond it. Warm air rolled out carrying the smell of woodsmoke, wet wool, and something coppery. Their father stepped inside. Mike swore and followed. JT remained in the snow. Every part of him told him to run. He could return to the lodge, if he kept southeast, if the weather held, if the woods allowed it. From inside, Dad called his name. Not loudly. Tenderly. JT entered. The cabin was larger within than without. A fire burned in a stone hearth. Along the walls hung dozens of rifles, all old, all rusted. At the center of the room stood a rough table. On it lay the caribou they had killed the day before. Its hide had been opened neatly from throat to belly. Its dark eyes watched JT. Steam rose from its exposed chest. “That isn’t ours,” Mike whispered. Dad stood at the animal’s head, touching its muzzle. The same expression had returned to his face: bright eyes, parted lips, reverence so complete it looked like terror. The caribou inhaled. Its ribs lifted. Mike staggered backward. “Jesus Christ.” Then something knocked from beneath the floor. Three slow blows. Dad smiled. “It followed us home,” he said. The caribou turned its head toward JT. Its mouth opened. For one insane second, JT expected it to speak. Instead, his father’s voice came from its throat. “You ought to quit making things up.” Behind JT, the door swung shut. Mike raised his rifle, but the barrel trembled between the animal and their father. JT saw that neither of them looked at him. Neither seemed to remember he was there. That, more than the breathing carcass or the knocking beneath the floor, decided him. JT backed toward the door. The caribou’s eyes followed. He found the latch, lifted it, and threw himself outside. There was no snow. No burn. No cabin. JT stood alone in the clearing from the day before. Evening had come, though by his reckoning it should have been noon. The massive buck waited at the far edge, still as a carving. Behind him stood only trees. “Dad?” JT called. The woods leaned close around the word and swallowed it. The buck lowered its head—not threateningly, but almost in greeting. Then, from somewhere beneath JT’s boots, his brother began to knock.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric control while delivering genuine psychological horror. The author successfully maintains the established voice and perspective, keeping JT as the focal point while building tension through his isolation and uncertainty about reality. The prose style effectively balances grounded realism with surreal elements. Descriptions like "snow had begun ticking against the windows" and "little white seeds blown sideways out of a sky the color of old dishwater" create vivid imagery without becoming overwrought. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character dynamics, building tension, and advancing the plot. The story structure builds methodically from subtle unease to outright horror. The progression from unexplained tracks to the impossible cabin to the final reality-bending conclusion feels earned rather than arbitrary. The author skillfully uses familiar horror tropes (abandoned cabin, mysterious tracks, family secrets) while subverting expectations through the reality-shifting elements. Character actions remain believable within the established supernatural context. JT's responses feel authentic for a young person caught in an incomprehensible situation, while his father and brother's behavior becomes increasingly unsettling in ways that serve the narrative. The family dynamics ring true, particularly the subtle tensions and unspoken hierarchies. The horror elements work on multiple levels - the immediate supernatural threat, the psychological uncertainty about what's real, and the deeper existential dread of being abandoned by family members who no longer recognize him. The ending's circular structure, returning JT to the clearing while trapping his family elsewhere, provides a satisfying yet disturbing conclusion. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the supernatural elements could have been more subtly introduced, and a few instances where the prose becomes slightly dense. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements, maintaining the established tone while advancing the story through meaningful character development and escalating supernatural elements. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 90.0
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
At six in the morning, the screaming began. Lucifer opened one eye. The screaming continued, thin and persistent, rising through nine basalt floors from the Vestibule of Unlicensed Torment. It had the quality of a kettle that resented water. He reached across the bed and slapped the brass speaking tube. “Not before seven.” The screaming stopped. A moment later, it resumed at a respectful volume. Lucifer lay in the enormous bed and looked at the empty half beside him. Lilith’s pillow remained there, although she had not, technically, lived in the palace for four hundred and twelve years. It still held the slight hollow of her head. Lucifer knew this was impossible. The servants changed the sheets every Thursday, using linen spun from the burial shrouds of saints. He preferred not to examine the matter. There came a grinding sound from the throne room. “Cerberus,” he called. The grinding stopped. Lucifer counted to three. It started again. He dressed in a black suit tailored by a man who had once charged interest to widows. The cut was excellent. The man had been sent somewhere unpleasant, but not for the interest. In Hell, one had to maintain standards. Cerberus was under the Iron Throne, chewing its left front leg. All three heads looked up. One looked guilty, one defiant, and the middle one continued chewing. “Drop it.” The middle head swallowed. The throne leaned slightly to port. Lucifer stared at the hellhound. Cerberus wagged his tail, knocking over a bronze urn containing the ashes of a minor prophet. “Do you know what that throne cost?” Cerberus sneezed sparks. “It was forged from the weapons of every tyrant who mistook fear for loyalty.” The left head whined. “Yes, including several collectible pieces.” There was a knock on the throne-room doors. Before Lucifer could answer, they opened, and Lilith walked in carrying two paper cups of coffee. She wore a red coat over a black dress. Her hair moved in a wind that existed for her alone. Four courtiers in the corridor turned to stone from reflex. A fifth, recently appointed, merely fainted. “I was nearby,” she said. Lucifer looked at the cups. “You were nearby a café?” “Don’t be childish.” “We are nine circles below creation.” “They deliver now.” She handed him a coffee. His name had been written on the side as *Lou Cipher*. Beneath it, someone had drawn a smiling devil. Lucifer sipped. It was exactly right. Lilith glanced at the throne. “He’s eaten more of it.” “He’s anxious.” “He’s spoiled.” Cerberus crawled from beneath the throne and placed all three enormous heads against her knees. She scratched behind six ears without looking down. “Traitor,” Lucifer said. “I came about the eastern rebellion.” “Of course.” “And to return your book.” “Of course.” “And because your messages have become embarrassing.” “I sent one message.” “You sent a raven at midnight.” “It was a memorandum.” “It was wearing a little bow tie.” Lucifer looked toward the doors. “Administrative whimsy improves morale.” Lilith smiled into her coffee. By eight, the Court of Torments had assembled. The agenda included a border dispute between the demons of envy and covetousness, a shortage of boiling pitch, and a petition from the damned requesting that Tuesdays be abolished. Lucifer sat on the damaged throne. It rocked whenever he shifted his weight. Lilith occupied the chair beside him, though no one had asked her to. No one ever did. Asking Lilith to sit was like granting permission to winter. The first petitioner was Buer, President of Several Things, who rolled forward on five goat legs. “My lord,” he said, “the Department of Despair has exceeded its quarterly lamentation targets.” “Then reduce the targets,” said Lucifer. Buer blinked. “Would that not be dishonest?” Every demon in the room turned to stare at him. Buer coughed. “I withdraw the question.” Lilith leaned close to Lucifer. “You’re slouching.” “I am the Adversary.” “You can be the Adversary with lumbar support.” “The throne is compromised.” “Because someone won’t discipline his dog.” Cerberus, lying at the foot of the dais, covered his ears with his paws. At ten, rebellion arrived. The eastern insurgents stormed the palace armed with hooked blades, banners, and the conviction that they had nothing to lose. This was an odd conviction to bring into Hell, where the inventory of losses was extensive and carefully indexed. Their leader pointed his sword at Lucifer. “Your reign ends today!” Lucifer looked at his appointment book. “I can give you twelve minutes.” The battle lasted nine. Lilith killed no one, strictly speaking. She simply persuaded the rebels’ shadows to attack their owners. Lucifer watched her work with the distant professional admiration of a man remembering why he had once given her the keys to the abyss. When it was over, she wiped a drop of black blood from his cheek with her thumb. “You missed one.” “I was distracted.” “By what?” “You.” “That line was old when fire was invented.” “It worked then.” “It did not.” “You married me.” “You fell from Heaven. We were both making impulsive choices.” At lunch, they ate sandwiches in the palace kitchen while damned kings roasted in the ovens. Cerberus sat between them, receiving alternate pieces of meat. “You’re making him beg,” said Lucifer. “He has three mouths and no income.” “He guards the gates.” “He sleeps at the gates.” “He creates an atmosphere.” “So does mildew.” Lucifer gave Cerberus another slice. “You said my messages were embarrassing.” “They are.” “You kept the raven.” “It bites the postman.” “I could send another.” “I’ll kill it.” “You said that about the first one.” Lilith’s expression softened, then caught itself. The kitchen clock struck one. Somewhere, a sinner discovered that eternity had no lunch break. In the afternoon, Lucifer inspected the new punishment complex. The architect, a twitching Florentine, explained that each chamber had been designed around the victim’s deepest regret. “This one,” he said proudly, opening a door, “contains an endless dinner with a former lover, during which both parties insist they are doing wonderfully.” Lucifer stared inside. A couple sat across from each other beneath flattering light. “You look well,” said one. “So do you,” said the other. Silence accumulated like snow. Lilith, beside Lucifer, said, “Too cruel.” The architect beamed. “Thank you.” By evening, the palace had quieted to its customary moan. Lilith stood at the doors with her coat over one arm. “Well,” she said. “Well,” said Lucifer. Cerberus lay between them, chewing something. Lucifer looked down. The hellhound froze. Protruding from his middle mouth was the last intact claw-foot of the Iron Throne. “Drop it.” Cerberus swallowed. From the throne room came a vast metallic groan, followed by a crash that shook dust from the ceiling. A chorus of courtiers screamed. Then, uncertain whether this had been scheduled, they applauded. Lilith laughed. It was not a cruel laugh, though she had many of those. It was the laugh Lucifer remembered from before kingdoms, before blame, when the world had been young and nobody had invented divorce. “You could stay,” he said. She raised an eyebrow. “Until the throne is repaired,” he added. “For administrative continuity.” “Of course.” “And the eastern districts remain unstable.” “Naturally.” “And the dog misses you.” Cerberus rolled onto his back. Lilith considered them: the fallen angel, the shameless beast, the ruin of imperial furniture. “One night,” she said. Lucifer nodded with regal gravity. At midnight, the screaming began again. Lilith opened one eye. Lucifer reached for the speaking tube, but she caught his wrist. “Let it ring,” she murmured. So they did.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing the prompt's requirements. The author successfully creates a "day in the life" narrative that seamlessly blends the mundane with the infernal, achieving the requested dark comedy through understated irony. The domestic relationship drama between Lucifer and Lilith is expertly woven into the administrative business of Hell, creating absurd juxtapositions that feel both natural and surprising. The Gaiman influence is evident in the dry, matter-of-fact tone used to describe extraordinary circumstances, and the way bureaucratic Hell mirrors earthly institutions. The prose is elegant and controlled, with excellent pacing that builds through small moments to emotional payoff. Dialogue feels authentic and reveals character efficiently - Lilith's sharp wit and Lucifer's wounded pride come through clearly in their exchanges. Character development is nuanced, particularly in showing the complex dynamic between the protagonists. Their relationship feels lived-in and real despite the fantastical setting. Lucifer's loneliness and pride, Lilith's independence and hidden affection, are conveyed through action and subtext rather than exposition. The imagery is vivid without being overwrought - details like the throne made from tyrants' weapons and coffee cups with misspelled names create a rich world efficiently. The recurring motif of Cerberus destroying the throne provides both comedy and metaphor for the relationship's instability. The ending earns its moment of tenderness through the careful buildup of small gestures and shared history. The transformation from "off again" to tentative reconciliation feels organic rather than forced, grounded in character truth rather than plot convenience. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the cleverness slightly overshadows emotional truth, and some descriptions that border on being too polished for the understated tone. However, these are minimal compared to the overall achievement. Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 1.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first thing I smelled each morning was bread. Not the black loaves we ate in the ludus, dense as roof tiles and full of grit, but white bread baking beyond the walls, in shops that opened before dawn to feed Rome. Its warm scent drifted through the bars of our sleeping room and mingled with lamp smoke, old straw, sour wine, and the liniment rubbed into our bruises. For a few breaths, with my eyes shut, I could imagine I was free. Then someone turned over on the pallet beside mine, and his chain scraped the floor. “Up,” called Marcellus from the corridor. He struck his staff against a doorpost. “The emperor does not wait for sleepy men.” Neither, I thought, did bakers, undertakers, tax collectors, or gods. Rome alone possessed the arrogance to pretend the whole world kept her hours. I sat up. My left shoulder ached from drills the day before, though the arena physician had pressed it with clever fingers and declared it sound. Around me, men coughed, muttered, and pulled on woolen tunics. Light seeped through the high window, gray and thin. Above my pallet, scratched into the plaster, was a small figure with a winged helmet and a staff. Mercury looked more like a long-legged spider than a god, but I touched two fingers to him anyway. “Watch over thieves and travelers,” I whispered, “and men who are both.” I had been a traveler first. A mule driver’s son from the hills above Lugdunum, I had followed the legions east as a wagoner. Then came dice, debt, and a foolish attempt to run from a creditor whose brother kept records for the governor. Rome had laws enough to make any misfortune appear orderly. By the time I reached the capital, I wore an iron collar. In the yard, the city was waking beyond our walls. Cart wheels clattered over stone before the daytime ban drove heavy traffic from the streets. Donkeys brayed. A water seller cried his wares. Somewhere nearby, a woman was already arguing at full volume, her words rising above the roof tiles like startled pigeons. We ate porridge with figs and a pinch of salt. Barley mush steamed in my bowl, smelling faintly of ash. Cassian, a veteran murmillo with half an ear, chewed slowly across from me. “They posted the program,” he said. I kept my eyes on my breakfast. “Did they?” “You’re listed for the seventh day.” The spoon stopped halfway to my mouth. Around us, benches scraped and men laughed too loudly. No one looked at me. In a gladiator school, privacy was another luxury reserved for citizens. “Against whom?” Cassian shrugged. “A Thracian from the imperial school, they say.” The imperial games had become more frequent. There were victories to celebrate, anniversaries to flatter, grain shortages to make people forget. The emperor’s officials called such spectacles gifts, though the people paid for them through taxes, conquest, and blood. Rome had been uneasy all summer. The Tiber warehouses stood guarded after crowds accused merchants of hoarding grain. In the Subura, someone had painted a senator with a rat’s tail. The picture vanished by noon, along with the painter. “Seven days,” I said. “Six, once today is gone.” Cassian smiled without humor. After breakfast, Marcellus sent me with two others to the bathhouse attached to the school. It was not kindness. A lanista protected his property from infection as carefully as a moneylender protected his tablets. The bathhouse air wrapped around me, hot and wet. Water dripped from the vaulted ceiling. Men’s voices echoed through steam, and the floor burned pleasantly beneath my feet. An attendant scraped my skin with a bronze strigil, drawing away oil, dust, and yesterday’s sweat in gray curls. Through an open lattice I saw a wedge of Rome: red roofs, pale columns, laundry strung between upper windows. Beyond them rose the vast curve of the amphitheater, its arches bright in the morning sun. It looked less like a building than a mountain fashioned by slaves. The attendant followed my gaze. “Beautiful, isn’t it?” “From here.” He snorted. He was a narrow-faced boy, perhaps fifteen. A faded auction mark showed on his neck. “My mother sells chickpeas outside the eastern entrance,” he said. “She says business is better when there are executions.” He spoke without accusation. That made it worse. Later, we were marched to the armory for inspection. The corridor smelled of leather, metal polish, and the sharp vinegar used to clean wounds. Helmets sat in rows, their bronze faces empty. Shields leaned against the wall like doors to houses no one wished to enter. Our trainer examined my teeth, eyes, knees, and shoulder. He made me raise both arms. “Pain?” “No.” It was a lie, but a small one. Rome was built from small lies mortared together: that conquered peoples welcomed order, that the Senate still governed, that slaves were naturally servile, that a man who fought well could earn his freedom. Some did. Cassian had collected four wooden counters toward the sum required to buy his release. He carried them in a leather pouch and touched them whenever officials visited, as if they were protective charms. Yet each year the price increased. When the inspection ended, I was permitted an errand under guard. My sandals struck warm paving stones as we entered the street. Rome engulfed me at once. Perfume drifted from a shop, thick with roses and myrrh. Beside it, a fuller’s doorway reeked of urine. Fish sauce, frying onions, dung, incense, wet plaster, and human bodies blended beneath the sun. Litters pushed through pedestrians. Lawyers hurried toward the Forum with slaves carrying bundles of scrolls. A senator passed in a spotless toga, broad purple stripe gleaming, while a beggar with clouded eyes held out both hands. On a wall, freshly painted letters praised the emperor as father of the world. Beneath them, someone had scratched: THEN LET HIM FEED HIS CHILDREN. Our guard saw me reading and jabbed my ribs. At a crossroads shrine, I stopped. Mercury stood in painted clay, one foot lifted, purse in hand. Someone had left him olives, a copper coin, and a tiny wax sandal. I had nothing except the piece of fig saved from breakfast. I placed it before him. “You stole that from the kitchen,” said the guard. “Then Mercury will value it more.” He laughed despite himself. I prayed silently—not for victory, because prayers should not insult the intelligence of a god. I asked for quick feet, a clear mind, and a door where others saw a wall. Mercury had escaped Apollo’s anger with lies and a lyre. Perhaps he might teach me the tune. As we turned back, a crowd gathered around a herald reading an edict. Grain prices were to be fixed. Hoarders would be punished. The emperor, in his boundless generosity, had ordered a public distribution. The listeners cheered, but an old woman beside me muttered, “They promise bread after taking the oven.” Then she saw my guard and lowered her eyes. At the ludus gate, Cassian waited in the shade. His expression told me something had changed. “A visitor,” he said. “A woman from Lugdunum.” For a moment, the street’s noise receded. All I heard was the flutter of pigeons above the tiles. “My sister?” “I don’t know.” Marcellus called my name from within. I touched Mercury’s scratched symbol hidden beneath my tunic, where I had copied it onto my skin with charcoal. Hope rose in me, painful as a reopened wound. Six days remained before the games, but suddenly the seventh seemed less certain than the next hour. I stepped through the gate, and it closed behind me with the soft, final sound of a lock turning.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and historical authenticity in depicting a gladiator's daily life in ancient Rome. The author successfully avoids the obvious combat scene, instead crafting an intimate portrait through mundane activities that reveal deeper truths about the character and his world. The sensory details are particularly well-executed, from the opening contrast between white bread's aroma and the harsh reality of "black loaves...dense as roof tiles," to the bathhouse's humid atmosphere and Rome's cacophony of street sounds. The author demonstrates solid research, incorporating authentic details like strigils, the daytime cart ban, and social hierarchies without overwhelming the narrative. The protagonist emerges as a nuanced character - a former mule driver's son turned gladiator through debt and poor choices. His voice remains consistent throughout: resigned yet observant, bitter but not self-pitying. The scratched Mercury symbol and his prayers reveal vulnerability beneath stoic acceptance. His background as a traveler who became both thief and prisoner adds complexity to his identity. The political and social commentary integrates naturally through observations about grain shortages, imperial games as distractions, and the growing unease in Rome. The graffiti about the emperor feeding his children and the old woman's muttered complaint about bread promises effectively capture popular discontent without heavy-handedness. However, the piece suffers from some predictable elements. The mysterious visitor from Lugdunum feels like an obvious plot device, and the ending's focus on renewed hope, while emotionally appropriate, lacks surprise. Some descriptions occasionally veer toward purple prose, particularly in passages about Rome's contradictions and the amphitheater as a "mountain fashioned by slaves." The dialogue, while limited, feels authentic to the period and characters. Cassian's laconic delivery of bad news and the bathhouse attendant's matter-of-fact observation about executions ring true. The narrative maintains good pacing, moving smoothly between reflection and action without becoming meandering. The piece succeeds in feeling like part of a larger story, establishing the gladiator's world and relationships while building toward the upcoming games. The emotional complexity - hope mixed with resignation, small dignities within dehumanization - creates genuine engagement without sentimentality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**The Lighthouse Keeper** **14 February 1998** **Muir Fhada Lighthouse** Dear Mam, Arrived Tuesday by the board’s boat. The sea was behaving, though Mr Tait says not to trust it for long. The lighthouse sits beyond the Butt of Lewis, on a slab of black rock where the Atlantic comes in with its sleeves rolled up. There’s a narrow path to the cottages and nothing taller than thrift grass. You can hear the blowhole at Sìthean Mòr thumping under the cliffs. It sounds like somebody shifting furniture in the flat below. My room is clean enough. I’ve put your photograph in the drawer because Tait stares from the doorway when he talks, and I don’t want him seeing everything. The work is simple: polish the lens, check the diesel, log the weather, wind what needs winding. I can do simple. Don’t worry about me. Your son, Calum **20 February 1998** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, I was not worrying until you told me not to. Put the photograph out. That is what photographs are for. If Mr Tait asks, tell him your mother is the handsome one and you take after your father. Mrs Dalrymple says lighthouse keepers go peculiar from staring at the horizon. I told her factory packers go peculiar from staring at biscuit tins, yet nobody writes poems about us. Are you eating? “Clean enough” is not an answer. Love, Mam **3 March 1998** **Muir Fhada** Mam, We eat. Tait makes stew with no fixed ingredients. Yesterday there was mutton in it, or boot. The lamp turns beautifully. You should see the prisms catch the afternoon sun. Every wall fills with small rainbows, even the scullery. Then at dusk the great beam starts its rounds: two flashes every twenty seconds. That is us saying, *Here is the rock. Keep clear.* I like that there’s a use to it. The photograph is on the mantel. Calum **12 March 1998** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, Good. I have enclosed socks. Don’t tell me you have socks. These are better socks. Your old supervisor phoned about shifts at the yard. I said you were employed elsewhere. He said, “For how long?” in that nasty soft voice of his. I said, “Longer than you’ll be supervisor, if there’s justice.” Write more than three lines next time. Mam **28 April 1998** **Muir Fhada** Dear Mam, Tait has gone ashore for dental work, so I’ve had eleven nights alone. The board says this is not standard, but nobody volunteered for the relief. There are noises the building makes. I know most now: the stair rods ticking as the temperature drops, the panes flexing, the wire stays humming. Last night I heard knocking below the kitchen. Three knocks, pause, two knocks. I went outside with the torch. Nothing there but spray. Before you write it: yes, I know there are pipes. Calum **5 May 1998** **Dumbarton** Calum, There are pipes. Also gulls, loose shutters, and your imagination, which was never small. When you were seven, you refused to sleep because you believed the wardrobe was breathing. It was your father’s raincoat moving in the draught. You have always listened too hard when frightened. That is not criticism. Listening is your job now. Just remember to look as well. Mam **19 June 1998** **Muir Fhada** Mam, A yacht lost its mast north of Sìthean Mòr. I saw the flare at 02:17 and radioed Stornoway. Lifeboat got three people off. Tait said, “Good eye,” and made tea. That was all. I couldn’t hold the mug properly after. One of them sent a note. A woman called Elise. She wrote that the flashes kept them from drifting onto the reef. I’ve read it six times. At the yard, if I did something right, nobody drowned. Here, if I miss something— I slept badly. Calum **25 June 1998** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, You did not miss it. There is no sense punishing yourself for all the nights that have not happened. Your father did that. Every silence became a redundancy notice; every cough became cancer. He suffered each disaster in advance, then was too tired when ordinary life came asking. Keep Elise’s note. Read it once, not six times. I am proud of you. I hope that does not make you uncomfortable, but I’m too old to arrange my sentences around your discomfort. Love, Mam **2 September 1998** **Muir Fhada** Mam, Tait retires in November. They’ve offered me senior keeper. More money, though not much. Longer stretches. I said I’d think. The nights are drawing in. Fog for four days. The horn shakes dust from the ceiling. Sometimes I find myself speaking aloud just to hear a voice answer from the walls. I could come home. There may be work at the bottling plant. Calum **9 September 1998** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, Come home if you want to come home. Do not come because you think I need minding. My hip complains, but so do I, and nobody has suggested replacing me. You hated the yard. You came through the door each night with your jaw clenched. At least the lighthouse gives you rainbows in the scullery. But loneliness is not wages. Ask what relief they will provide. Ask twice. Working men are expected to accept hardship as if it proves something. It generally proves only that management saved money. Mam **18 November 1998** **Muir Fhada** Mam, I took the post. I also made them agree to radio calls every evening and relief every four weeks. Tait said he’d never asked for either. I said maybe he should have. He laughed until he coughed. He leaves Friday. I’m sorry to see him go. Strange, considering we’ve exchanged perhaps thirty sentences since February. I’ve bought a little tape player. Send me something with voices. Not music. People talking. Calum **26 November 1998** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, Enclosed: two tapes. Tape One is me reading the *Dumbarton Reporter*, including advertisements, because you asked for people talking and must endure the consequences. Tape Two is Mrs Dalrymple describing her operation. Destroy after listening. Mr Tait sent me a postcard. It says: “He’ll do.” I assume that means you. Mam **7 January 1999** **Muir Fhada** Mam, The generator failed during the storm. I got the reserve going, but the fuel line clogged after twenty minutes. Had to climb to the lantern room with the wind pushing through the seals, strip the line, clean it, prime it. Light was out for eleven minutes. Eleven. A cargo vessel radioed to ask our status. I could hear the officer trying not to sound alarmed. When the beam came back, he said, “Muir Fhada, good to see you.” Afterward I was sick in the sink. I keep thinking of Dad. Not his dying. The night before, when he asked me to sit, and I said I had an early shift. I thought there would be another chance. There wasn’t. Calum **13 January 1999** **Dumbarton** My dear Calum, Your father knew you loved him. He also knew you had an early shift because he had worked early shifts for thirty-eight years. I have never told you this: after you left, he said, “The boy’s done with watching me be ill.” He was not angry. He was glad you still belonged to tomorrow. You could not keep his light burning. That was never your duty. Eleven minutes is not abandonment. You repaired what failed, alone, in a storm. Then you were sick because you are flesh, not machinery. Please come home on your next relief. I would like to see your face rather than hear it through tape hiss. Love, Mam **4 February 1999** **Dumbarton** Calum, You missed the boat, they say. Weather. Telephone when you can. Mam **6 February 1999** **Dumbarton** Calum, No evening radio call. The board says the aerial may be down. They say the light is operating. I know that should comfort me. Write. Mam **8 February 1999** **Muir Fhada** Mam, Safe. Lightning took the aerial and part of the roof. The landing cove is smashed with swell, so nobody gets on or off yet. The lamp is running. I’ve been sleeping in the service room because the bedroom ceiling leaks like a sieve. I heard the knocking again last night. Three, pause, two. This time I looked properly. A loose length of drainpipe under the floor, striking a joist. You were right in May. Don’t become unbearable about it. Calum **12 February 1999** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, I shall become exactly as unbearable as the circumstances merit. The board telephoned. They say automation is coming next year. No permanent keeper after that. You did not mention this. Mam **20 February 1999** **Muir Fhada** Mam, I didn’t know how. When they switch it over, the light will still turn. Sensors will report faults. A helicopter will bring engineers. Better, probably. Safer. Cheaper. I thought losing the job would feel like the yard closing: men swearing, lockers emptied, somebody calculating what your years were worth. Instead it feels like leaving a room where I finally learned how to stand. Calum **1 March 1999** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, A room can teach you something without owning you forever. Come home when it closes. Not to mind me. I have applied for a council flat with two bedrooms, and I refuse to waste one on Mrs Dalrymple’s sewing machine. There is work maintaining the navigation equipment along the Clyde. I cut out the notice. It asks for diesel experience, electrical aptitude, and “ability to operate independently in exposed locations.” That sounds suspiciously like you. The horizon is not only in Lewis. Mam **17 October 1999** **Muir Fhada** Mam, Last week before automation. I’ve cleaned every prism. Painted the rail. Logged the weather in my best handwriting. Tonight the sea is flat enough to show the beam lying across it, a road that appears and disappears. I used to think the light meant, *Keep clear.* Now I think it means, *You are not the only one awake.* Interview on the Clyde is Tuesday. I’ll be there, weather permitting. Calum **2 November 1999** **Dumbarton** Dear Calum, Your boots are in the hall, your mug is beside the sink, and the spare room smells of damp wool. This is therefore a formal complaint: after nineteen months of letters, you have come home and still leave me notes. Congratulations on the job. I found the photograph from the lighthouse mantel in your coat pocket. Your father and me at Balloch, both squinting into the sun. Put it on your own mantel now. You were never meant to stay on that rock. You were meant to learn that darkness can be worked through—sometimes with a lens, sometimes with a spanner, sometimes by answering your mother. There is soup on the stove. Come through when you wake. Love, Mam
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional depth. The story follows Calum, a Scottish lighthouse keeper, through correspondence with his mother over nearly two years, chronicling his journey from isolation and self-doubt to acceptance and growth. The adherence to instructions is excellent. The piece maintains the epistolary format throughout, spans the required timeframe, features distinct voices for both characters, builds to an emotional climax around the generator failure and Calum's guilt about his father, and concludes with satisfying resolution. The Scottish working-class voice is authentic without relying on heavy dialect or stereotypes. Character development is particularly strong. Calum's voice evolves from terse, defensive communications to more open, reflective writing. His mother emerges as pragmatic, loving, and wise without being saccharine. Both characters feel fully realized through their distinct writing styles and concerns. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery that avoids purple prose. Descriptions like "the Atlantic comes in with its sleeves rolled up" and "the blowhole... sounds like somebody shifting furniture in the flat below" are vivid and grounded in Calum's perspective. The technical details of lighthouse keeping feel authentic and serve the narrative rather than overwhelming it. The emotional arc is well-constructed. The story builds tension through Calum's isolation, the responsibility of his work, and unresolved grief about his father. The climax during the generator failure effectively brings these threads together, and the resolution feels earned rather than forced. The mother's voice provides excellent counterpoint - practical, caring, and gently challenging Calum's tendency toward self-punishment. Her letters reveal her own depth and history without overshadowing Calum's journey. Minor weaknesses include some predictable elements in the arc (the automation announcement, the job offer) and occasional moments where the wisdom feels slightly too polished for natural correspondence. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The ending achieves genuine warmth without false positivity. Calum's growth feels authentic, rooted in his experiences and his mother's guidance. The final image of him leaving notes even when home provides a perfect character detail that shows change while maintaining continuity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
