+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I notice the camera before I notice her. It’s mounted above the restaurant entrance, angled too high, capturing foreheads and the top halves of umbrellas. A decorative vine obscures the left third of its field of view. The cable runs exposed along the brick and disappears through a hole sealed with what looks like chewing gum but is probably old silicone. I tell myself not to assess it. Then I assess the host stand. Tablet unlocked. Reservation list visible. Staff PIN written under the lip of the counter on fluorescent tape. Four digits. Possibly a year. The Wi-Fi password is printed at the bottom of the cocktail menu, and the network name is the restaurant’s name plus “Guest,” which suggests there’s also a network without “Guest,” which suggests someone believed nomenclature counted as segmentation. Stop. You are not working. You are meeting a woman named Lena who likes old science-fiction movies, volunteers at a community garden, and has described herself in three separate messages as “aggressively punctual.” She is seven minutes late. Not suspicious. Traffic exists. Buses exist. Human beings are not distributed systems with service-level agreements. At minute eight, the door opens, and there she is—green coat, silver earrings shaped like tiny swords, hair damp from the rain. The swords are from something. I know they’re from something. If I identify them immediately, will that seem observant or alarming? “Sorry,” she says. I almost say, *No problem, I know exactly when you arrived.* Instead: “I just got here.” Lie. Harmless lie. Social encryption. We sit. She puts her bag on the chair beside her rather than hanging it from the back. Good situational awareness, though probably incidental. Her coat pocket bulges with a rectangular object—phone, outer edge facing up. Easy to lift in a crowd. Stop profiling your date. The conversation starts with safe ports open: neighborhood, weather, the restaurant’s tiny chairs. She jokes that the chair is “an ergonomic hate crime.” I laugh too hard, then worry I’ve produced the laugh of someone simulating normality. She asks what I do. “Cybersecurity.” True, but polished to remove sharp edges. Her eyebrows go up. “So you’re a hacker?” There it is. The word people say with delight, as if I carry grappling hooks and neon code. “White hat now.” Why did I add *now*? “Now?” Because apparently I enjoy placing conversational bear traps beneath my own feet. “I was stupider when I was younger.” “That’s not exclusive to hackers.” Good answer. No recoil. No follow-up asking whether I’ve been arrested, which is usually less about concern and more about wanting a story. The waiter brings menus. Lena glances at the wine list and says, “I can never remember whether I like Malbec or Merlot. My dad was the wine person.” Breadcrumb one: father, past tense maybe, wine enthusiast. Don’t collect it. “My parents had a bottle from the year I was born,” she continues. “Ninety-two. They finally opened it when I graduated from Oberlin.” Breadcrumbs two and three: birth year, college. They land in my head and organize themselves without consent. 1992. Oberlin. Father associated with wine. Possible security questions: year of birth, first school, college, father’s middle name if we get there. Credential recovery systems love biography because biography feels private to people who tell it to strangers over appetizers. Ask about the food. I ask whether she wants to split the mushrooms. She says, “Only if you’re okay with me eating seventy percent.” Negotiation. Clear threat model. I like her. Then I notice the swords again. “Are those Glamdring and Orcrist?” She touches an earring. “Close. Blackblade Reforged.” My brain stops scanning the room. “Magic?” “Since middle school.” A door opens. Not a technical door. A better one. “What format?” “Commander, mostly.” “Of course.” “What does that mean?” “It means you enjoy friendship until someone plays a board wipe.” She grins. “Says the blue player.” “I don’t play blue.” “You have the energy.” This is going well. Alarmingly well. Within minutes we’ve abandoned respectable first-date topics and begun arguing about whether proxy cards are ethically superior to making adults spend grocery money on cardboard. She tells me her first deck was green-white tokens, assembled with her cousin Mira at a kitchen table in Ann Arbor. Breadcrumb four: cousin’s name. Five: hometown or childhood location. Six: first hobby. I try not to retain them. My mind retains them. She once named a cat Jace, which she regrets. Her childhood best friend was Natalie. Her elementary school mascot was a badger. Her mother’s maiden name is unusual, “like the bird, but with two Ns,” though she doesn’t actually say it because the waiter arrives. Thank you, waiter. The mushrooms arrive. She takes exactly seventy percent. I’m wondering whether liking someone is just discovering that their weirdness interoperates with yours when her phone vibrates. She lifts it from the table. I look away. I genuinely look away. Then peripheral vision betrays me. She traces an unlock pattern: top left, center, bottom right, bottom middle. Four nodes. Simple angled line. Visible smudge trail. Anyone shoulder-surfing from the next table could reproduce it. I could reproduce it. A child behind us probably could. Don’t say anything. She checks the message. Don’t say anything. She puts the phone down. You can let this go. “That pattern is really insecure.” Silence. Not dramatic silence. Worse. The small, clean silence after glass breaks in another room. Lena looks at her phone, then at me. I hear myself continuing, because apparently the first shovel strike didn’t reach the coffin. “It’s only four points, and it forms a common geometric sequence. Plus the screen has a visible trace.” Stop. “You watched me unlock my phone?” “I saw it.” “That’s not better.” Correct. Accurate finding. “I wasn’t trying to—” “That is exactly what someone trying to would say.” Also correct. Her posture changes. Shoulders back. Bag closer. The date’s attack surface contracts. I could explain attentional capture, occupational conditioning, years spent finding weak authentication before criminals do. I could tell her I once prevented a hospital from exposing thousands of patient records because I noticed a login portal using sequential reset tokens. I could explain that my brain tags vulnerabilities the way hers might identify birds. None of that changes the fact that she is a person, not an engagement. “I’m sorry,” I say. Keep it clean. No justification disguised as apology. “I shouldn’t have commented on it like that. And I shouldn’t have kept looking once I realized what I’d seen.” She studies me. I resist the urge to fill silence. Silence is not a system error. Finally: “Do you do this all the time?” “Yes.” Bad answer. I amend it. “Notice things? Yes. Say them? I’m trying not to.” “How’s that going?” I glance at the phone. Her mouth twitches despite itself. Possible recovery vector: humor. Dangerous if deployed too early. “I’d rate tonight as a failed penetration test.” No. Absolutely not. Do not say— “I’d rate tonight as a failed penetration test.” Her eyes widen. Sabotage achieved. Then she laughs. One sharp burst she tries to swallow. “That,” she says, “was the worst possible phrase.” “I knew before I finished saying it.” “Did you?” “Halfway through. But by then the packet was sent.” Another laugh. Smaller, but real. The threat level drops from *leave immediately* to *conditional monitoring*. She picks up her phone. “Fine. What should I use?” This is a trap, or a gift, or both. “A longer PIN. Six digits minimum. Not your birthday, graduation year, address, or anything someone could infer from conversation.” Her expression shifts. I can see her replaying the evening: 1992. Oberlin. Ann Arbor. Mira. Natalie. Badgers. Jace. “Oh my God,” she says. “I’ve basically handed you my entire password-reset profile.” “Not your first car.” “Blue Honda Civic.” I close my eyes. She laughs again. “Sorry. Couldn’t resist.” “Please don’t tell me your childhood street.” “Rosewood Lane.” “You’re doing this on purpose.” “My first pet was Sprinkles.” “Lena.” “My mother’s maiden name—” “No.” There. A boundary. Late, but functioning. She changes the pattern, shielding the screen with her other hand. Good. She tilts it away from me with exaggerated suspicion. Better. Then she asks, “What’s your Commander deck?” This is either forgiveness or evidence collection. “Mono-black sacrifice.” “I knew you were a villain.” “Reformed villain.” “White hat.” “Gray wardrobe.” She looks at my charcoal sweater. “Suspiciously gray.” The date resumes, not repaired exactly, but patched. Patches aren’t elegant. They are admissions that something broke and effort followed. I still notice the waiter’s terminal is running an outdated operating system. I notice Lena’s new PIN is six digits. I deliberately do not notice which ones. That feels like progress. When the check comes, she says, “Next time, bring a deck.” Next time. Two-factor authentication for hope: words plus smile. I leave the restaurant beside her, resisting the urge to point out the useless camera. Some vulnerabilities do not require exploitation. Some doors are more meaningful when you wait to be invited through.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative understanding of the prompt. The author successfully captures the mindset of a reformed hacker through consistent internal monologue that reveals both technical expertise and social awkwardness. The cybersecurity terminology is used naturally and authentically, creating believable character voice. The narrative structure works well, building tension through the protagonist's inability to stop analyzing vulnerabilities, culminating in the phone pattern observation that creates genuine conflict. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest provides effective character bonding and feels organic rather than forced. The prose is generally elegant with clever metaphors that blend technical and human elements ("Social encryption," "packets," "attack surface contracts"). The internal monologue maintains consistent voice throughout, effectively showing the protagonist's analytical mind constantly cataloging information. Character development is nuanced - the protagonist is simultaneously sympathetic and problematic, aware of their issues but struggling to control them. Lena's reactions feel authentic, showing appropriate wariness followed by cautious forgiveness. The dialogue, while limited, serves the story well. The technical banter feels natural, and the awkward moments land effectively. The "failed penetration test" line is genuinely cringe-worthy in the intended way. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The ending feels slightly too neat - Lena's forgiveness and invitation for a second date may be somewhat unearned given the severity of the privacy violation. The protagonist's self-awareness sometimes borders on being too articulate about their own flaws. The pacing occasionally slows with excessive technical detail, though this generally serves character development. Some metaphors, while clever, occasionally feel slightly forced in their technical precision. The emotional complexity is strong - the protagonist's genuine desire for connection conflicting with ingrained professional habits creates compelling internal tension. The piece successfully balances humor with genuine social anxiety and the real consequences of boundary violations. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6.0, Unearned Transformations: 5.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The projector squeaks every time Ms. Patel flips to the next slide. Not a normal mechanical click. A squeak. High and rubbery, like a sneaker twisting on a gym floor. Each time it happens, half the class ignores it, because apparently they possess the supernatural ability to ignore minor noises. I do not. I anticipate it. “Today you’ll be working in pairs—” Squeak. “—to build a model demonstrating heat transfer.” Squeak. Beside me, someone taps a pencil against a desk. Behind me, Ava whispers that she hopes she gets Jordan. I hope I don’t get anyone, which is not how pairs work. There are twenty-eight students in this room. Statistically, the chance of getting paired with one specific person is low. “Lena, you’ll work with Mateo.” Statistically, I am dead. I keep my face neutral. Neutral means interested but not alarmed. It means I have heard the information and accept it as ordinary, because it is ordinary. Mateo is just a person. A person whose smile makes one side of his mouth lift before the other. A person who pushes his hair away from his forehead when he’s thinking. A person whose lab notebook is covered with tiny drawings of planets. I have known none of this on purpose. “Okay,” I say. Too fast. Too eager. Or maybe normal. People say okay when assigned partners. I should not say anything else to correct it. Correcting it would make it strange. Okay, but less excited. No. The moment has passed. Squeak. We drag our chairs together. Mine scrapes loudly enough to register on weather equipment. “Sorry,” I mutter. Mateo glances at the floor. “For what?” Excellent. I have apologized for moving furniture. Now he knows I am the kind of person who assumes chairs have feelings. “The noise.” “Oh.” He smiles. “It’s a chair. I think it’ll recover.” I should laugh. Not too much. A small laugh. Air through the nose, perhaps. But if I only breathe through my nose, he might think I didn’t find it funny. If I laugh loudly, he’ll think I’m trying to flatter him. Desperate girls laugh at everything. I have seen movies. I produce a sound like “huh.” He opens the instructions. Say: So, do you want to handle the insulation part while I sketch the model? Clear. Practical. Demonstrates competence without domination. “So,” I begin. He looks up. My brain evacuates. “Do you…” I stare at the worksheet as if the sentence is printed there. “Want to… do it?” Do it. Perfect. A phrase with no specific meaning. “The project?” he asks. “Yes. Not all of it. Obviously. I meant—” Stop speaking. “—like, part.” He nods slowly. “Sure.” I tuck my hair behind my ear. Was that a normal hair movement or a deliberate-looking hair movement? People tuck their hair behind their ears when flirting. People also tuck their hair behind their ears when hair is in their faces. Mine was not in my face. He may have noticed. Do not touch your hair again for the rest of class. Mateo reads the materials list. “We need cardboard, foil, cotton, and two thermometers.” “I can get them.” Too eager again. “I mean, unless you want to.” Now I sound unwilling. “We can both go,” he says. Of course. Both. Because we are partners. We walk toward the supply table, and I become conscious of my arms. What do arms usually do? Swing, but not dramatically. Mine feel borrowed. I try to let them move naturally, which makes them move unnaturally. Mateo reaches for the foil at the same time I do. Our fingers do not touch. This should be a relief. It is somehow disappointing, which is ridiculous. This is science class, not a scene where violins appear because two people almost touch aluminum foil. “Sorry,” I say. Again. He hands me the roll. “You apologize a lot.” My entire body becomes hot. This is the moment. I could say, Only when I’m nervous, and smile. That would be honest but dangerously revealing. He might ask why I’m nervous. Then I would have to answer because existing near you has turned basic language into an obstacle course. Alternative: I’m Canadian at heart. No. Weird. Also I’m not Canadian. Alternative: Sorry. Absolutely not. “I guess,” I say. Brilliant. Back at our table, Mateo draws the box while I measure cardboard. His lines are crooked. “I’m bad at straight lines,” he says. Say: Good thing I’m here, then. Playful. Confident. Potentially flirtatious, but deniable. “Lines are hard,” I say. Lines are hard? He probably thinks I struggle with geometry. Squeak. “Remember,” Ms. Patel says, “your model needs two different insulating materials.” Mateo leans closer to compare our worksheet with the slide. I stop breathing. This is unsafe. Not physically. Biologically, perhaps, because oxygen is important. His sleeve brushes the table near my wrist. He smells like laundry detergent and pencil shavings, which should not be a good combination, yet here we are. Ask him something casual. Do you like science? Too boring. What are you doing this weekend? Too obvious. Sounds like I am planning to invite myself. I like your notebook drawings. Too observant. He’ll wonder how often I look at his notebook. Your planets are accurate. Worse. Sounds like I have audited his planets. He points at our materials. “Cotton or foam?” “Cotton,” I say. “It traps air between the fibers, reducing conduction.” A full sentence. A real one. The words arrive because this is information, and information does not look back at me waiting to decide whether I am weird. “Whoa,” he says. “You actually understand this.” He means it as a compliment. Probably. Unless “actually” means he assumed I didn’t understand anything, which seems unlikely because I got the highest score on the last test. Unless he didn’t notice. I should respond gracefully. “Only because I read the chapter.” That sounds like bragging about reading. “It’s not complicated.” That could make him feel stupid. “Thanks.” Just say thanks. “Kind of,” I say. Why am I like this? He grins. “Kind of thanks?” “No. I mean—yes. Thanks.” My face is smiling. Is it too much? Reduce smile by fifteen percent. Now I may look annoyed. Increase slightly. Stop adjusting your face like it has settings. Mateo cuts the cardboard, nearly slicing through our data table. “Careful,” I say. “I’ve got it.” He does not have it. I hold down the edge. Our hands are close again. I focus on the corrugation in the cardboard: parallel channels, evenly spaced, predictable. My heart behaves like a trapped moth. I could ask if he wants to meet after school to finish the model. That is a normal project question. Except we could finish during class. Asking would expose an ulterior motive. He would hear it instantly: Hello, would you like to spend unnecessary time with me while I pretend cotton insulation requires a private conference? Maybe he’ll ask me. He probably won’t. Maybe that means he doesn’t like me. Maybe it means he thinks we can finish now, which we can. Maybe he likes someone else. Ava, probably. Everyone likes Ava because she speaks without mentally reviewing every syllable in three separate committees. Mateo looks up. “You okay?” I have been holding the same piece of cardboard for thirty seconds. “Yes.” Say something more. “I was thinking.” “About the project?” “Yes.” Lie. Technically adjacent to the project. Squeak. “Ten minutes,” Ms. Patel announces. Mateo groans. “We’re not finishing.” Here. Opportunity. Do not ruin it. “We could work on it after school.” The sentence exits intact. My pulse fills my ears. I add, “If you want. Or not. We could split it up. That might be easier. I don’t care.” I care so much I may dissolve. Mateo considers this for one second, which lasts a semester. “After school works,” he says. “Library?” “Sure.” Neutral. Good. He tapes foil inside the box, smiling faintly. Maybe at the project. Maybe at nothing. Maybe at me, though that possibility is too bright to examine directly. I place the cotton along the edges and try not to smile too obviously. Library after school. Two people completing an assigned task. Entirely ordinary. I will only need to plan what to say when I arrive, where to sit, whether to bring snacks, whether bringing snacks seems desperate, how long to make eye contact, and approximately six hundred other things. The projector squeaks again. For once, I barely hear it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional skill in capturing the authentic voice and psychological landscape of an anxious teenage girl. The writer masterfully executes the challenging task of creating an almost entirely internal monologue that remains engaging and dynamic throughout. The adherence to instructions is nearly flawless. The narrative successfully presents an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety, places her in the specified group project scenario, and maintains the requested focus on internal monologue while exploring her crush on Mateo. The piece effectively demonstrates the gap between her articulate internal voice and her stumbling external expression. The character work is particularly strong. The protagonist's voice feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending or cartoonish. Her overthinking patterns are rendered with psychological accuracy - the way she anticipates the projector's squeak, analyzes every micro-interaction, and cycles through potential responses before settling on inadequate ones. The internal committees reviewing her syllables and her mental calculation of smile percentages capture the exhausting hypervigilance of social anxiety perfectly. The prose demonstrates considerable elegance and control. Sentences like "My pulse fills my ears" and "that possibility is too bright to examine directly" show sophisticated metaphorical thinking. The recurring projector squeak serves as both a realistic detail and a structural device that punctuates her anxiety. The writer shows excellent restraint, avoiding overwrought emotional displays while still conveying the intensity of the protagonist's feelings. The dialogue, though minimal, serves its purpose well. Mateo's responses feel natural and age-appropriate, while the protagonist's stilted external speech contrasts effectively with her fluid internal voice. The awkward exchanges ("Do you want to... do it?") ring true to the social fumbling of adolescence. The piece maintains strong coherence throughout, with each moment building naturally from the protagonist's psychological state. The ending strikes an appropriate note - hopeful but not unrealistically transformed, acknowledging that her anxiety will continue while allowing for a small victory. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal analysis becomes slightly repetitive, and some metaphors that border on being too polished for the character's age, though these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The battery is at four percent, which feels deliberate. Not the battery’s fault, obviously. I know that. Lithium-ion cells do not conspire. Still, the red sliver in the corner of the screen looks accusatory, like even my phone has had enough of carrying my emergencies. Four percent. Mara hasn’t replied for thirty-seven minutes. She said, *See you at eight. Looking forward to it.* No heart. Usually there’s a heart. Not always. But usually enough that the absence has weight. I stand in front of the mirror wearing the black shirt she once said made me look “dangerously pretty,” and immediately hear what she’ll say tonight. *I think things got too intense.* Of course. Fine. I’ll nod. I’ll be elegant about it. “I understand,” I’ll say, while arranging my face into something calm and tragically mature. “You don’t owe me anything.” Then she’ll look relieved. That will be the worst part: relief spreading across her face because I’ve made leaving easy. So I’ll add, “I just wish you’d been honest instead of slowly withdrawing.” She’ll say she wasn’t withdrawing. I’ll list the evidence. Tuesday: six hours to answer. Wednesday: no goodnight text. Friday: canceled drinks because of a headache. Today: thirty-seven—now thirty-eight—minutes of silence, no heart. She’ll tell me I’m keeping score. “I keep score because you keep changing the rules.” Good. That’s good. I should remember that. Except she hasn’t changed any rules. We never made rules. We slept together three times, then spent Sunday eating noodles in her bed while she traced circles on my shoulder and said she was glad we’d met. She called me the next morning. She has been busy all week. She warned me she’d be busy all week. *Busy is what people say when they’re trying to leave without having to watch you understand that they’re leaving.* Three percent. I shove the charger into my bag, though the café might not have an outlet. Maybe I should stay home. If I stay home, I can say my phone died. Then she can experience uncertainty for once. That thought gives me a quick, poisonous relief. Yes. Let her wonder. Let her check the door every time it opens. Let her feel stupid for looking forward to something. But she said she was looking forward to it. Maybe she means looking forward to ending it cleanly. I can see the whole evening: her already seated, hands wrapped around a glass, expression tender in the way people look when they’re about to hurt you but want credit for kindness. *You’re wonderful.* I hate “wonderful.” Wonderful is the ribbon they tie around rejection. *You’re wonderful, but I’m not in a place—* “I didn’t ask you to be in a place.” *This is exactly what I mean. You turn everything into a fight.* “I’m responding to being ambushed.” *You’re making me responsible for your feelings.* “You are responsible for your actions.” There. Another good line. I pull on my coat. My chest is tight, but beneath the fear there is something hardening—preemptive anger, clean and useful. If I’m angry, I’m not begging. If she’s selfish, I’m not unlovable. If I decide she has already abandoned me, then whatever happens is merely confirmation, not injury. I know this pattern. I actually think the words: *I know this pattern.* For half a second, the machinery becomes visible. My mind is building a courtroom so I won’t have to enter a café. Prosecutor, witness, defendant, all played by me. Mara hasn’t said any of it. That should matter. It matters for half a second. Then my phone vibrates. Two percent. *Running five late. Sorry! I got you something. Don’t let me forget x* An x. Not a heart. An x can be typed automatically. “Got you something” could mean she’s returning my earring. It could be my toothbrush. Did I leave a toothbrush? I didn’t. Maybe she bought one to make some point about boundaries. That makes no sense. Or it’s a gift. The wanting rises so fast it humiliates me. She got me something. She thought of me somewhere I wasn’t. For a moment I am flooded with warmth so intense I have to sit on the edge of the bed. Then suspicion pours over it. Guilt gift. Consolation prize. Two percent. I type, *No worries, see you soon x*, delete the x, put it back, delete “soon” because it sounds needy, restore it because without it I sound cold. The phone dies before I can decide whether the message sent. Of course. On the walk, I rehearse being casual. “Hey.” No, too flat. “Hi, you.” Too intimate. I imagine her face falling when she sees me, because I have arrived carrying all this invisible weaponry. She’ll know. People always know eventually. At first they think I’m intense, funny, perceptive. Then they realize perception is just what I call scanning every change in temperature for proof of disaster. At the café, Mara is standing outside. She sees me and smiles—not politely, not tightly. Her whole face opens. “There you are.” She steps forward and wraps both arms around me. I freeze for one beat, because this was not in the script. Then I hold her so hard I worry I’m revealing everything. “I missed you,” she says into my hair. The words enter me like water after thirst. My body believes them instantly. My mind does not. She missed me. She says that because she knows she’s been distant. She noticed she was distant. She feels guilty. Or she missed me. Inside, she hands me a small paper bag. “I saw this and thought of you.” It’s a tiny ceramic crow with one gold foot, ridiculous and solemn. Two weeks ago I told her I wanted a council of ugly bird figurines on my windowsill. She remembered. The fact is enormous. It glows in my hand. “I love him,” I say, and my voice breaks slightly. Mara touches my wrist. “Hey. You okay?” Here it comes. I could say, *I’ve spent the past hour defending myself against things you never said.* Instead: “Phone died. Stressful journey.” “You should’ve told me. I have a power bank.” Of course she does. Prepared, stable Mara, carrying spare electricity for people who fail to manage their own lives. “I know that face,” she says softly. My stomach drops. “What face?” “The one where you’ve decided I’m criticizing you.” I look at her sharply, ready to deny it, then accuse her of patronizing me, then leave before she can leave. But she reaches across the table, palm up. “I’m not,” she says. “I’m sorry I’ve been quiet. This week was brutal. It wasn’t about you.” Exactly what someone would say if it were about me. Also exactly what someone would say if it wasn’t. I place my hand in hers. She squeezes. “I really like you. I’m not disappearing.” The relief is almost painful. It rushes through me so violently that tears press behind my eyes. I want to climb inside the sentence and live there. I want it notarized. I want her to repeat it every hour forever and never resent me for asking. “You can’t promise that,” I say. Her thumb moves over my knuckles. “I can promise I’m here tonight.” Tonight. Only tonight. No—tonight is real. This is the part I am supposed to practice: accepting what is actually happening. Her hand is warm. The crow is on the table between us. She is looking at me, not the door. I breathe. For perhaps ten seconds, I let the evidence exist without cross-examining it. Then she glances at her phone. My mind sits upright. Who is that? Is she bored? Is there someone else? I feel my hand begin to withdraw, preparing punishment, preparing distance, preparing to survive. Mara turns the screen toward me. “Power bank instructions. Your phone’s charging in my bag.” “Oh.” She smiles. “You’re allowed to stay reachable.” The tenderness of it nearly destroys me. I smile back, and even as love floods the space where fear was, I search her expression for the first sign that she regrets it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of borderline personality disorder and the internal experience of anticipatory rejection. The writer successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone caught between desperate need for connection and preemptive self-protection. The narrative structure is well-executed, moving seamlessly between the protagonist's catastrophic predictions and reality. The phone battery metaphor works effectively as both a practical plot device and symbolic representation of the narrator's depleted emotional resources. The internal monologue feels authentic, capturing the rapid cycling between hope and despair characteristic of BPD. The prose is generally strong, with moments of genuine insight like "Wonderful is the ribbon they tie around rejection" and "My mind is building a courtroom so I won't have to enter a café." These lines demonstrate both literary skill and psychological accuracy. The writer shows rather than tells the protagonist's condition through specific behaviors like counting minutes between texts and analyzing punctuation choices. The characterization of both the narrator and Mara feels believable. Mara's responses are realistically patient without being saintly, and her small gestures (the ceramic crow, the power bank) feel genuine rather than contrived. The narrator's brief moments of self-awareness ("I know this pattern") followed by immediate return to catastrophizing accurately reflect the limited insight common in personality disorders. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being slightly overwrought in its emotional intensity. Some metaphors, while effective, border on purple prose. The ending, while psychologically accurate in showing the immediate return to hypervigilance, could be seen as somewhat predictable given the established pattern. The dialogue, though minimal, serves its purpose well. Mara's words feel natural and the narrator's imagined conversations are distinct from the real ones. The cognitive dissonance between expectation and reality is well-maintained throughout. The piece succeeds in creating genuine empathy for someone whose thought patterns might otherwise seem irrational, making it both emotionally engaging and educational about the internal experience of BPD. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 8.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Physical-spatial: Blood Debt — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
"Blood Debt" Write a vivid third-person narrative depicting an unsanctioned underground duel between two talented but hot-headed martial artists. Their schools have been embroiled in a generations-old rivalry, and despite strict prohibitions from their masters, these two younger fighters arrange a secret confrontation fueled by personal pride and simmering animosity. - **One fighter** represents **Jigen-ryū**, a traditional, aggressive style practiced historically by elite samurai clans. Its practitioners emphasize explosive offense, decisive strikes, and overwhelming speed. Known historically for their arrogance and disdain toward outsiders, practitioners consider their art superior and untouchable. - **The opposing fighter** employs a form of historical **Piguaquan**, a Chinese martial art traditionally practiced by commoners, bodyguards, and street fighters. This style emphasizes whipping, circular strikes, rapid footwork, unpredictable angles of attack, and evasive maneuvers. Practitioners often come from lower social classes and view the traditional elite as pretentious and detached from true combat effectiveness. The fight takes place in the crumbling ruins of an abandoned temple, now functioning as a hidden arena for illicit duels. Passionate supporters from both factions surround them, wagering money, pride, and reputation on the outcome. **Essential Instructions:** Maintain absolute physical coherence throughout—clearly track fighters' positions relative to each other and their environment. Precisely depict strikes, counters, momentum shifts, and spatial dynamics, allowing readers to clearly visualize the mechanics and choreography of the fight at every moment. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Blood Debt The abandoned temple crouched beneath the hillside like a broken jaw. Its southern wall had collapsed inward, leaving a mound of stone beneath a roof open to the night. The old altar stood against the north wall, split through the middle and blackened by rain. Between them stretched a rectangular floor of cracked flagstones, twelve paces long and eight wide. Spectators crowded its edges, pressed shoulder to shoulder beneath sagging galleries and roofless alcoves. A single torch burned in an iron bracket on the eastern wall. Its flame leaned whenever wind entered through the southern breach, throwing the fighters’ shadows across the west wall—one rigid and spear-straight, the other loose-limbed and wavering. At the northern end stood Akira Shindō of Jigen-ryū. He wore a dark practice jacket, sleeves tied back, and held a blunt-edged training katana in both hands. Blunt was a courtesy of language. The oak-hard steel could break ribs, crush a throat, or split a skull. Akira’s right foot rested half a pace behind his left. The sword rose beside his right shoulder, blade angled upward. His weight pitched forward, every line of him prepared to become one descending stroke. Across from him, near the southern rubble, Liang Wei rolled his shoulders. He carried no weapon. His faded gray shirt hung open at the chest, and cloth wraps covered his hands. His left foot pointed toward Akira; his right stood back and outward. Unlike Akira’s compressed stillness, Liang seemed carelessly mobile. His spine flexed. His arms swung with the loose weight of ropes. Coins changed hands along the western edge. “First clean surrender,” called the bookmaker from beside the torch. “Or inability to continue.” Laughter answered him. Everyone knew there would be no surrender. Their masters had forbidden this duel. Akira’s teacher had called Piguaquan “market-square flailing.” Liang’s uncle had called Jigen-ryū “a rich man’s method for killing someone who must stand still.” Then Akira had broken Liang’s younger brother’s jaw outside a sake house. Liang had spat on the gate of the Shindō school. Blood had not yet been spilled, but the debt had grown interest. Akira moved first. His left foot stamped forward. The impact cracked through the temple as his sword plunged from his right shoulder toward Liang’s left collarbone. There was no probing cut, no concealment—only acceleration, both hands driving, hips and shoulders committed behind the blade. Liang did not retreat toward the rubble. He stepped diagonally forward and right, toward the torchlit eastern side, slipping outside the sword’s path. The blade passed his left shoulder by a hand’s breadth and struck a flagstone where he had stood. Before Akira could lift it, Liang’s right arm whipped in a downward circle. His forearm struck Akira’s hands, forcing the sword’s tip against the floor. Liang’s left palm lashed toward Akira’s ear. Akira released his lower hand, raised his left forearm, and blocked. The palm slapped bone. He drove his forehead forward. Liang folded backward at the waist. Akira’s brow missed his nose, but Akira’s free hand caught Liang’s shirt. He hauled Liang upright and thrust the sword hilt toward his ribs. Liang rotated clockwise, turning his left side away. The pommel grazed his flank instead of landing squarely. Continuing the turn, Liang swung his right arm around like a flail. The back of his fist struck Akira behind the left ear. Akira staggered two steps west. The western spectators scattered from his path. He recovered before reaching them, seized the sword in both hands, and swept horizontally from left to right. Liang was now near the center, with Akira west of him and the torch to his right. He dropped below the sweep. The blade hissed over his hair. From the crouch, he surged toward Akira’s exposed right side, planting his left foot outside Akira’s right foot. His left hand slapped Akira’s elbow forward; his right palm hammered the lower ribs. Akira grunted but did not yield ground. He reversed the sword immediately, dragging the blade back right-to-left at head height. Too close to duck, Liang stepped behind Akira’s right shoulder. The returning blade passed in front of Liang’s chest. Liang hooked his right arm over Akira’s sword arm and struck twice with his left hand: palm heel to the kidney, chopping forearm across the back of the neck. Akira dropped to his right knee. The Piguaquan faction roared. Liang tried to wrench the sword arm backward, but Akira rolled over his right shoulder toward the north. The motion pulled Liang off balance and freed the trapped arm. Akira came up kneeling three paces from the altar, sword point aimed at Liang’s abdomen. Liang, standing between Akira and the southern breach, halted. Blood ran from Akira’s ear. He smiled. “You strike like a washerwoman.” “And you bleed like a lord.” Akira rose. This time he advanced in short, stamping steps, denying Liang space. Liang gave ground southward, circling to his own left. Akira turned with him, keeping the point centered. The crowd compressed along the edges as the fighters’ axis shifted: Akira now faced southeast; Liang backed toward the fallen stones in the southern breach. Liang’s rear heel touched rubble. Akira saw it. He lunged, thrusting for the chest. Liang knocked the blade inward with his left forearm while stepping left, but a loose stone rolled beneath his right foot. His deflection came late. The point tore through his shirt and scored a red line across his right pectoral. Akira converted the thrust into an upward diagonal cut. Liang leaned away. The blade clipped his chin and snapped his head back. He stumbled east, toward the torch wall. Akira pursued with the terrifying certainty his school prized. He raised the sword high and stamped into another descending cut. Liang’s shoulders touched the eastern wall. There was nowhere behind him. As Akira struck, Liang slid left along the wall. The blade crashed into the torch bracket. Sparks burst. The bracket bent downward, and the torch dropped between them. The flame landed on the stones, still burning, its light now low and wild. Shadows leapt to the ceiling. Akira’s sword had rebounded toward his right. Liang stepped inside before he could recover. He caught Akira’s right wrist with his left hand, folded his body, and whipped his right arm upward from hip to jaw. The palm heel landed cleanly. Akira’s head snapped back. Liang pivoted counterclockwise around their joined hands, moving from Akira’s front to his right side. His right arm circled down across Akira’s elbow while his left hand twisted the wrist outward. Akira lost the sword. It clattered beside the fallen torch. Liang kicked it westward. The weapon slid beneath the feet of the spectators. Akira answered with his body. He drove his right shoulder into Liang’s chest and carried him north three paces. Liang’s back struck the cracked altar. Before he could slip aside, Akira punched him in the abdomen, then again beneath the sternum. Air fled Liang’s lungs. Akira seized his throat with his left hand and drew back his right fist. Liang let both arms fall loose. Akira mistook looseness for defeat. Liang’s right arm swung outward, down, then up in a full circle, knocking Akira’s gripping arm away. Simultaneously he stepped off the altar line to his left—west—and planted his right foot behind Akira’s left heel. His left palm struck Akira’s chest while his right forearm whipped across the jaw. Akira toppled backward over Liang’s blocking leg. His shoulders hit first. His skull struck the flagstones a heartbeat later. Silence swallowed the temple. Liang stood above him, facing south, one foot on either side of Akira’s left arm. Akira tried to rise. His elbows trembled, then collapsed. His eyes remained open but unfocused, reflecting the fallen torch. Liang drew back his heel above Akira’s throat. Around the arena, Jigen-ryū men reached inside coats. Piguaquan supporters lifted clubs and chains. The old rivalry seemed to inhale. Liang looked down at the man who had broken his brother’s jaw. Then he lowered his foot to the floor. “The debt is paid,” he said. Behind him, the torch guttered in a pool of its own resin. Darkness climbed the walls, covering the wagers, the weapons, and the faces of men disappointed that pride had not demanded a corpse.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical craft in depicting martial arts combat with precise spatial awareness and choreographic clarity. The author meticulously tracks fighter positions, environmental details, and the mechanics of each exchange, fulfilling the prompt's demanding requirement for "absolute physical coherence." The setting of the ruined temple is vividly established with economical but evocative details - the collapsed southern wall, cracked altar, and single torch create both atmosphere and tactical elements that influence the fight. The prose style is lean and muscular, avoiding flowery language while maintaining strong imagery. Sentences like "The abandoned temple crouched beneath the hillside like a broken jaw" establish tone without overindulgence. The author effectively contrasts the two fighting styles through both description and action - Akira's rigid, explosive approach versus Liang's fluid, adaptive movement patterns. Character motivations are clearly established through the backstory of broken jaw and spat-upon gate, creating believable animosity. The fighters' personalities emerge through their combat styles and brief dialogue exchanges, though character development remains necessarily limited by the action-focused scope. The fight choreography is the piece's greatest strength. Each strike, counter, and positional shift is clearly described with attention to physics and spatial relationships. The progression feels natural - neither fighter dominates completely, and the momentum shifts feel earned rather than arbitrary. The environmental elements (rubble, torch, altar) are integrated meaningfully into the combat. The ending demonstrates restraint and thematic coherence. Liang's decision to spare Akira feels consistent with the established "blood debt" concept rather than representing an unearned transformation. The final image of disappointed spectators adds cynical depth. Minor weaknesses include limited dialogue and some repetitive sentence structures in action sequences. The piece occasionally borders on technical manual precision at the expense of emotional resonance, though this serves the prompt's specific requirements well. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 17
